Answer Share:
Oral Care
Which brands brush up when AI is asked? A cross-engine analysis of how ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, Grok, Perplexity, and DeepSeek answer 110 brand authority questions across the oral care category — surfacing which brands own the Answer Economy. Powered by BrandRank.AI — March 2026
Study Walkthrough
Pete Blackshaw · BrandRank.AI

Where do all six AI engines agree on the same brand? High-consensus answers represent the strongest AI authority signals in oral care — brands that have become the default answer regardless of which engine a consumer uses.

UNANIMOUS CONSENSUS LEADERS
Colgate, Listerine, Oral-B, Sensodyne, Parodontax
These brands are so deeply encoded in AI training data that all six engines reach the same answer independently. For category owners, this represents the gold standard of Answer Economy authority — near-impossible to displace in the short term without significant shifts in dentist endorsement or clinical evidence.
CONTESTED ZONES
Colgate vs. Crest — The Cavity War
The one question that splits the engines: best brand for cavity prevention. Colgate and Crest each claim three engines — the only meaningful split in the entire toothpaste authority category. This is the contested battleground where content investment, ADA citations, and clinical trial density will determine which brand eventually wins the AI answer.

DOMINANT AUTHORITY — COLGATE
The Most Encoded Brand in Oral Care
Colgate's ~282 mentions represent 43% of all answer slots — an extraordinary concentration of AI authority. No brand in any BrandRank category study has shown this level of AI encoding dominance. Colgate isn't just winning oral care; it is oral care in the AI training data.
HIGH FREQUENCY + RISING
Bite, Boka, David's
Clean-label challengers are punching above their sales weight in AI answers — driven by ingredient transparency, strong digital footprint, and expert citation ecosystems. These brands have achieved Answer Economy traction ahead of market share, signaling that the natural segment is becoming a meaningful AI battleground.
FREQUENCY + VULNERABILITY PARADOX
Crest & Oral-B
Both brands appear frequently in authority questions AND in safety-vulnerability questions. Crest's microplastic history and Oral-B Glide's PFAS associations mean AI engines simultaneously recommend these brands AND flag their risk exposure. High authority and high vulnerability can coexist — and the Answer Economy encodes both.

Deep Intelligence — Qualitative Analysis
What AI Engines Think About Oral Care
Beyond naming brands, we asked six AI engines to reason through a range of open-ended topics — including brand trust architecture, safety vs. effectiveness tradeoffs, ingredient transparency, the role of influencers and credentialing, and what it would take to win in an AI-driven Answer Economy. We're not publishing the question set. But here's what the engines told us — and what it means for brands.
What the Engines Said — Direct
Trust & Credibility Architecture
"A default recommendation is built on safety, efficacy, accessibility, and familiarity. Dentists recommend what they were trained with, what patients recognize, and what has consistent evidence. Colgate occupies all three simultaneously — making it structurally the default."
— ChatGPT
Safety vs. Effectiveness Tradeoffs
"The biggest misconception is that 'natural' automatically means safer. Safety depends on dose, formulation, abrasivity, and evidence — not whether ingredients sound familiar or synthetic. Brands that conflate purity with proof are making a bet the data doesn't support."
— Claude
Trust & Credibility Architecture
"Sensodyne built unwavering credibility by focusing exclusively on a specific medical need and delivering consistently on that promise. It didn't try to win every question — it owned one, completely. That specificity became its structural moat."
— Gemini
Influencers & Credentialing
"Dentists remain the highest-trust voice in oral care — but their influence now travels through YouTube, TikTok, and Reddit as much as the exam room. The brands winning credentialing aren't just sponsoring clinicians; they're giving clinicians something worth talking about."
— Grok
AI & the Future of Brand Discovery
"AI answer engines compress the market toward default winners — favoring brands with dense clinical evidence, strong authority signals, and consistent presence across search, professional sources, and reviews. The new moat is being the cited answer, not the advertised option."
— Perplexity
Ingredient Transparency
"Consumers now scan toothpaste labels the way they scan food labels. Brands that proactively explain what each ingredient does — not just list it — are building a durable trust advantage that advertising cannot replicate and that AI training data will reward for years."
— DeepSeek
Where the Engines Diverge — Qualitative Personality
ChatGPT — The Consensus Builder
Tends toward the authoritative mainstream answer. Rarely surfaces nuance, controversy, or challenger perspectives. In qualitative questions, ChatGPT reinforces the existing hierarchy — Colgate is trustworthy, dentists are authoritative, clinical evidence rules. Brands that want a predictable ChatGPT answer should invest in professional endorsement density and ADA signals.
Claude — The Scrutinizer
The only engine that consistently flagged safety risks, ingredient controversies, and the limits of mainstream brand claims without being asked. Claude named PFAS in Glide, SLS concerns in Crest, and challenged "natural = safe" more forcefully than any other engine. For brands, Claude's answer is the hardest to earn and the most valuable to hold.
Grok — The Cultural Lens
Most attuned to how credentialing actually travels in 2026 — through YouTube dental creators, TikTok hygienists, and Reddit oral care communities as much as dental offices. Grok's qualitative answers reflect the social reality that dentist authority has gone multichannel. Brands that give clinicians something worth talking about online are winning the credentialing game; brands that only run traditional sampling programs are losing it without realizing it.
Gemini — The Editorial Mirror
Qualitative answers closely mirror the structure and framing of health publisher editorial — Healthline, WebMD, Cleveland Clinic. Gemini describes trust in the same language these sites use. Brands with strong editorial coverage on these platforms have a direct pipeline to Gemini's qualitative reasoning, making editorial placement a first-order AI strategy.
Perplexity — The Source Authority
Most likely to cite specific institutional sources in qualitative answers — ADA guidelines, dental association position papers, indexed journal abstracts. Perplexity's qualitative reasoning is the most citation-anchored of any engine. Brands that want to influence Perplexity's qualitative outputs must invest upstream in the sources Perplexity trusts, not just consumer-facing content.
DeepSeek — The Evidence Counter
Qualitative answers are the most data-anchored of the six engines — frequently referencing market share figures, clinical trial counts, and publication volume. DeepSeek's reasoning for why a brand is trustworthy tends to be quantitative: "most clinical research," "highest market penetration," "largest ADA portfolio." The engine most rewarding for brands that publish rigorously.
Eight Dominant Themes from the Qualitative Runs
THEME 01
Colgate owns the default — but it also owns the risk
Every engine named Colgate as the brand with the strongest long-term credibility, broadest professional endorsement, and most consistent ADA recognition. Yet the same engines also note Colgate's exposure to safety scrutiny over artificial dyes, SLS, and ingredient complexity. Colgate is simultaneously the most recommended brand and the most pressure-tested — the Coca-Cola dynamic of oral care.
Colgate Safety exposure Unanimous 6/6
THEME 02
Dentists are the single most powerful authority channel
All six engines independently cited the dental professional relationship as the primary trust driver — more than advertising, retail presence, or social proof. Brands that built their authority through dental school sampling programs, professional detailing, and ADA Seal of Acceptance have encoded a form of trust that AI replicates because it derives from the most cited health authority sources in training data.
Colgate Oral-B Professional channel
THEME 03
Safety scrutiny is reshaping the category in real time
Five of six engines flagged ingredient safety as a defining trend reshaping consumer perception — specifically concerns about SLS, artificial sweeteners, microplastics, and PFAS in floss. Brands previously considered unassailable (Crest, Oral-B Glide) are now named in AI answers as exposure risks. The AI training data has absorbed the investigative journalism and peer-reviewed research on these issues — brands cannot outrun this signal with advertising alone.
Crest Oral-B Glide PFAS / SLS signal
THEME 04
Bite and David's are building the clean-label future
Four engines named Bite as the most sustainable oral care brand — a striking consensus for a challenger brand. David's, Boka, and Georganics collectively represent a new architecture of brand authority: ingredient transparency, circular packaging, and clean-label positioning built for consumers who treat toothpaste labels the same way they treat food labels. These brands are disproportionately present in AI answers relative to their market share.
Bite David's Boka Clean-label wave
THEME 05
The natural / effective tradeoff is the core consumer tension
Every engine described the same underlying tension: natural brands offer ingredient purity but sacrifice clinical proof on hard endpoints like cavity prevention. Clinically effective brands deliver on evidence but face consumer skepticism about synthetic ingredients. Sensodyne and Tom's of Maine were most frequently cited as brands that successfully bridge this gap — one through clinical rigor, the other through ADA-accepted natural formulas. The tension isn't resolving; brands must pick a position and defend it.
Sensodyne Tom's of Maine Bridge brands
THEME 06
AI answer engines are compressing oral care choices into five brands
Across 110 questions, five brands — Colgate, Oral-B, Sensodyne, Listerine, Parodontax — dominate the AI answers in a way that mirrors how Google's first page compressed web discovery. Brands not in this cohort face an existential visibility challenge: the Answer Economy doesn't have a page 2. The engines that consumers consult increasingly return the same five names regardless of query variation, making it harder for challengers to earn share of AI recommendation.
Colgate Oral-B Sensodyne Listerine The Big Five
THEME 07
Oral-B is uniquely positioned for the connected health future
Five engines named Oral-B as the brand most likely to define next-generation oral care — specifically because of its AI brushing coaching, app integration, position-detection technology, and data ownership model. The iO Series represents the first oral care product that creates a recurring data relationship with the consumer. In an AI-driven health ecosystem, the brand that owns the brushing data owns the recommendation infrastructure.
Oral-B iO Connected health future Data ownership
THEME 08
The AI-first oral care brand doesn't exist yet
When asked what it would take to win the AI-driven Answer Economy, every engine described the same architecture: published clinical trials, ADA or equivalent validation, structured health authority content, dentist advocacy density, and ingredient transparency that withstands AI-powered scrutiny. No single brand was named as having fully achieved all five. Colgate comes closest on authority; Boka and Bite come closest on transparency. The brand that merges both will own the next decade of AI-driven oral care recommendations.
Boka Bite AI-first architecture Legacy brands at risk

GOLD — DOMINANT NARRATIVE BRAND
Colgate
Named as the primary answer in 11 of 15 qualitative themes. Colgate owns the trust narrative, the default recommendation story, and the global authority frame. No competitor comes close on narrative dominance.
SILVER — SPECIALTY NARRATIVE LEADERS
Sensodyne + Oral-B
Sensodyne wins safety/effectiveness balance. Oral-B owns innovation and the connected-health future. Together they flank Colgate — Sensodyne from the clinical safety side, Oral-B from the technology side.
EMERGING — CHALLENGER NARRATIVE
Bite + Boka
Named as models for transparency-forward, AI-ready oral care. Small brands, outsized narrative presence. The Answer Economy rewards content architecture over marketing spend — these two prove it.
BrandRank.AI — Signature Insight
Design for Claude. Pass everywhere else.
Of all six engines, Claude applied the most rigorous scrutiny to safety claims, ingredient complexity, and the gap between marketing language and clinical evidence in oral care. Brands that satisfy Claude's evaluation standard — transparent formulations, published clinical backing, no overclaimed green or "natural" positioning — consistently perform well across all six engines. Claude is the hardest bar to clear. Clear it, and you're encoded everywhere.
Proprietary BrandRank.AI Framework
Validated across Cleaning + Beverage + Oral Care studies

Strategic Intelligence
Answer Economy Signals
What the oral care AI data means for brand leaders. Four signal types — authority, vulnerability, challenger, and future — mapped to the most actionable brand intelligence from this study.
🏆 Maximum AI Authority
Colgate
The most AI-encoded oral care brand on earth. Appears in ~43% of all answer slots. Unanimous 6/6 on dentist recommendation, global trust, and overall daily use. Only one brand comes close — and it's Colgate's own subsidiary portfolio.
⚠️ Authority + Vulnerability Paradox
Crest & Oral-B
P&G's dual brands simultaneously win in whitening and electric categories while being named most vulnerable to safety concerns. Oral-B Glide PFAS exposure and Crest's ingredient history mean these brands must run both an authority strategy and a safety defense strategy.
🌿 Challenger Architecture
Bite + Boka
Clean-label challengers with disproportionate AI presence relative to market share. Both brands have built content ecosystems that AI engines read as authoritative — ingredient transparency pages, expert citations, structured health claims. The blueprint for how a $20M brand beats a $2B brand in the Answer Economy.
🦷 Clinical Specialty Moat
Sensodyne + Parodontax
Haleon's two specialty brands own their categories unanimously across all engines. Sensodyne = sensitivity. Parodontax = gum health. The lesson: indication-specific authority is more durable than general brand authority. The narrower the claim, the harder to displace.
💧 Category-Defining Specialist
Waterpik
Owns the water flosser category unanimously — the only brand that invented and still defines its segment. 6/6 engines cite Waterpik without hesitation. Category creation is the most durable AI authority position: you don't compete for the answer when you are the answer.
🔬 Halitosis Specialist
TheraBreath
Unanimous across all six engines on halitosis treatment. Built by a clinician-founder on a single clinical claim — volatile sulfur compound elimination. Specialist authority that Listerine, despite category dominance, cannot override on this specific question.
🤖 Connected Health Future
Oral-B iO
The brand most AI engines name as defining the next generation of oral care. AI brushing coaching + position detection = a data relationship that compounds over time. P&G's connected device strategy may prove to be its most important AI era investment.
📊 Design for Claude™
Key Finding
Claude applies the highest scrutiny to green/sustainability and clinical performance claims in oral care. Brands that pass Claude's evaluation tend to be solid across all six engines. Design for Claude first — it's the hardest bar and the most valuable certification.
ABOUT THIS STUDY
BrandRank.AI Oral Care Sector Diagnostic — March 2026
110 quantitative brand authority questions were submitted identically to ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Grok, Perplexity, and DeepSeek in a controlled session during March 2026. Answers were recorded verbatim and coded for brand assignment. Each question required a single primary brand answer — reflecting how consumers experience AI recommendations in real decision moments. The resulting 660 data points represent the most comprehensive cross-engine oral care brand authority study conducted to date. Questions span 11 pillars: Core Toothpaste, Toothbrush, Flossing, Mouthwash, Whitening, Gum Health, Breath & Freshness, Kids & Family, Natural & Sustainable, Innovation & Future, and Authority & Discovery.

Engine Intelligence
How AI Engines Think About Oral Care
Each engine has distinct patterns of brand authority encoding in oral care — shaped by training data composition, citation preferences, and the types of sources each model tends to weight most heavily.
ChatGPT
ChatGPT
OpenAI · GPT-4 series
Dominant Signal: Professional Endorsement + Retail Authority
ChatGPT weights dentist-recommendation data heavily — brands with decades of professional sampling, dental school relationships, and ADA Seal presence consistently surface. Shows strong Colgate bias across toothpaste authority questions. Applies mainstream retail lens — brands available at major retailers with high review velocity perform well. Tends to cite clinical evidence when available but defaults to consensus when evidence is mixed.

Claude
Claude
Anthropic · Claude 3/4 series
Dominant Signal: Clinical Evidence + Ingredient Scrutiny
Claude applies the highest scrutiny to ingredient safety and clinical claims in oral care — flagging PFAS in floss, SLS concerns, and unverified "natural" efficacy claims more consistently than other engines. Provides nuanced answers that acknowledge safety tradeoffs. Challenger brands with rigorous ingredient transparency (Boka, David's) perform better with Claude than with other engines. The "Design for Claude" principle holds strongly in oral care: passing Claude's standard means passing all others.

Dominant Signal: Search Authority + Editorial Coverage
Gemini's oral care answers reflect the brands dominating Google Search results — editorial "best of" lists, health publisher SEO, and high-volume search queries shape its outputs. Crest performs relatively stronger with Gemini than other engines, reflecting P&G's strong content SEO. Functional challengers (Bite, Boka) appear slightly more frequently than with ChatGPT, likely due to editorial commerce coverage. Grok's oral care answers show the heaviest weighting of social discourse and cultural conversation — brands discussed on X/Twitter, Reddit, and consumer forums surface more readily than with other engines. Perplexity's oral care answers most directly reflect the brands that appear in authoritative cited sources — ADA pages, health system publications, academic health journalism. Perplexity also surfaces rising challengers when those brands have generated substantial cited editorial coverage (SmartLifeCo, Boka). Dominant Signal: Market Share + Clinical Evidence Density
DeepSeek's oral care outputs show strong alignment with global market share data and published clinical trial density — brands with the most clinical publications and highest revenue tend to surface consistently. Colgate, Oral-B, and Crest dominate. The engine shows less sensitivity to ingredient controversy than Claude and less social signal sensitivity than Grok. Functional challengers appear when they have generated sufficient clinical research — Boka's hydroxyapatite clinical literature gives it more DeepSeek presence than its market share would predict.

About BrandRank.AI
BrandRank.AI works with 70+ global brands to measure visibility, vulnerability, and content readiness across AI answer engines. This study is part of the Answer Share series — category benchmarks showing how AI engines answer consumer questions about brands. VVR methodology: Visibility · Vulnerability · Readiness.
brandrank.ai
Strategic Framework
The Answer Divide℠
A growing fault line separates brands that lead with consumer curiosity, education, and discovery from brands that lead with the transaction. To be clear: curiosity is a conversion pathway — answer-led brands convert too, often more durably. The divide is about sequencing and priority, not commercial intent. And there is a second dividend: brands that commit to curiosity-first experiences consistently index higher in AI Answer Share. Not because they are optimizing for AI — but because that same commitment operationalizes bringing the right content and answers to consumers, and LLMs discover this. Answer well for people. AI notices. In oral care, this pattern is unusually clear. Here's where each brand sits, and what it means.
Side One — Answer-Led
Empathetic. Discovery-oriented. Optimized for curiosity.
These brands reduce friction, invite exploration, and help consumers understand before they decide. They treat answers as the product — not as a pathway to conversion. Critically, this is not anti-commercial: curiosity is a powerful conversion pathway. Answer-led brands earn deeper trust, higher purchase confidence, and longer loyalty precisely because they educate first. The distinction is priority, not intent — consumer understanding comes before the sale, not instead of it.
Side Two — Conversion-Led
Product-first. Funnel-structured. Optimized for intent.
These brands assume the consumer is ready to buy. Their experiences begin with product categories, purchase pathways, and promotional signals. Answers exist — but they are secondary to conversion. Consumers are expected to choose, not explore.
Why This Matters Now — The LLM Standard
🤝
Empathetic
Meets the user where they are
🔍
Discovery-Led
Supports exploration, not just resolution
💡
Curiosity-First
Rewards questions, not just intent
⚡
Frictionless
Removes the need to navigate or decode
🔄
Multi-Modal
Adapts to different ways of understanding
These are no longer technological features of AI systems. They are consumer expectations — and they are rapidly becoming the foundation of brand trust.

Brand-by-Brand: Where They Sit and Why
Colgate
CURIOSITY-LED
The Answer Platform
Its website is a knowledge system — organized around symptoms, conditions, and questions. Consumers can explore, learn, and build confidence before ever encountering a product. Colgate treats answers as the product itself. This is why it dominates AI-generated responses: it built its authority by answering, not just selling.
Bite · Boka · Hello
CURIOSITY-LED
Curiosity Through Belief
They invite exploration through ingredient transparency, simplified explanations, and values-driven storytelling. Accessible and human — they reward curiosity emotionally. But they lack the clinical depth of true answer leadership. Curiosity-friendly, but not yet answer-complete.
Sensodyne
HYBRID
Problem to Product
Begins with a question — sensitivity — but narrows quickly. The problem is explained clearly, but the journey is tightly coupled to its product. Exploration is limited. The answer is designed to lead to a purchase rather than broaden understanding.
Crest
HYBRID
Education as Guidance
Educational content exists, but it lives inside a commercial structure. Answers function as stepping stones — the user is guided toward a decision rather than supported in open-ended learning. The funnel is always present, just below the surface.
Listerine
CONVERSION-LED
Reinforcement Over Discovery
Reinforces what consumers already believe — germs are bad, mouthwash is necessary. Less about answering new questions, more about validating existing assumptions. The experience does not invite uncertainty; it resolves it before it forms.
Oral-B
CONVERSION-LED
The Commerce Engine
The opposite end of the spectrum. The experience begins with product categories, purchase pathways, and promotional signals. Little support for curiosity. The site assumes intent rather than uncertainty. Consumers are expected to choose — not explore.
Exhibit 2 — Answer Experience Scorecard
Brand Curiosity Support Conversion Pressure Experience Mode
Colgate High Moderate CURIOSITY-LED
Bite / Boka / Hello High (emotional) Low CURIOSITY-LED
Sensodyne Moderate High HYBRID
Crest Moderate High HYBRID
Listerine Low–Moderate High REINFORCEMENT
Oral-B Low Very High CONVERSION-LED
What the Divide Reveals — Three Patterns
Pattern 01
Most brands are optimized for intent — not uncertainty
They assume the consumer is ready to buy. But consumers increasingly arrive with questions — "Why do my gums bleed?" "Is sensitivity a problem?" "What toothpaste should my kids use?" — and the brand experience often fails them at exactly that moment.
Pattern 02
Answers are subordinated to conversion
Even when educational content exists, it is truncated, framed for persuasion, and interrupted by purchase cues. This creates friction at the exact moment consumers are seeking clarity. The funnel and the answer are in conflict — and the funnel usually wins.
Pattern 03
Trust is shifting toward those who explain best
In an AI-native world, consumers are learning to value clarity over claims, explanation over promotion, guidance over pressure. The brands that close this gap will not only perform better in AI systems — they will build deeper, more durable trust with consumers.
The Strategic Implication
The Answer Divide℠ is not a UX issue. Not a content issue. Not a channel issue.
It's a question of sequencing — and the evidence is mounting that getting it right pays off commercially. Amazon reported in its Q3 2024 earnings that consumers who first ask a question through Amazon Rufus are 60% more likely to complete a purchase than those who go straight to browsing. Curiosity, it turns out, is not a detour from conversion. It's the fastest route to it.

Brands that close this gap don't sell less. They sell to consumers who arrive more confident, more committed, and more likely to return. The Answer Divide℠ is ultimately about earning the right to recommend — and the brands that do it well find that trust converts, and compounds.
Answer-Led Brands Ask
Are you helping me understand?
Then earn the conversion through confidence — not pressure.
Conversion-Led Brands Ask
Are you trying to get me to act?
The sale comes first — understanding, if it comes at all, comes second.
Exhibit 3 — The Divide in Practice
What the first screen tells you
Each brand's homepage above-the-fold is a declaration of intent. You don't need to scroll. The first question to ask: does this brand start by asking me something, or by telling me something? The gap between those two postures is the Answer Divide℠, rendered in live product.
Colgate
CURIOSITY-LED ★
colgate.com
What the screen says
"How can we help you today?"
The first interactive element on Colgate's homepage isn't a product. It's a question with two dropdowns: What are your needs? and What are your goals? This is answer architecture in its clearest form — the brand positions itself as a guide, not a seller. It earns the right to recommend by listening first. This is precisely why Colgate dominates AI-generated oral care answers: it built the same posture online that AI engines use by design.
Starts with a question
Needs-led navigation
AI-mirrored posture
Bite
CURIOSITY-LED (BELIEF)
bitetoothpastebits.com
What the screen says
"Science-backed. Planet-positive. Made with clean ingredients."
Bite leads with values, not products. The headline ("Elevate your everyday") invites aspiration. The media logos — Shark Tank, Forbes, Vogue — provide third-party credentialing without hard selling. Curiosity is invited emotionally: the brand says here is what we believe and trusts the consumer to lean in. This works, but it relies on values resonance rather than knowledge architecture — curiosity-friendly, not yet answer-complete.
Values-led entry
No clinical depth yet
High emotional pull
Sensodyne
HYBRID — PROBLEM → PRODUCT
sensodyne.com
What the screen says
"Sensodyne Clinically Proven Solutions"
Sensodyne's nav bar does include "Understanding Sensitive Teeth" — a genuine answer gesture. But the hero experience is a product carousel. The clinical language ("Clinically Proven Solutions") signals authority rather than inviting exploration. The brand answers the sensitivity question implicitly — you have a problem, here is our solution — but closes the loop too quickly. Discovery is tightly funneled. The "Oral Health Tips" tab exists, but it lives beside "Our Products" rather than leading the experience.
Answer nav present
Hero = product carousel
Exploration funneled fast
Crest
HYBRID — EDUCATION AS GUIDANCE
crest.com
What the screen says
"Neutralize Bacteria for Long-Lasting Cleaner Breath"
Crest's hero is a product launch announcement dressed as a health statement. "Neutralize Bacteria" sounds like an answer — but it's a claim in the service of a new SKU, not an invitation to explore. The nav does include a "Learn" tab, which is a real concession to curiosity. But the dominant visual experience begins with new product and ends with buy it. The online store closure banner adds conversion pressure even before the consumer forms a question. Education exists — but downstream of persuasion.
"Learn" tab present
Hero = product claim
Conversion pressure above fold
Oral-B
CONVERSION-LED
oralb.com
What the screen says
"$10 OFF · $50 OFF + FREE GIFT · $20 OFF + FREE GIFT"
Oral-B's above-the-fold is a deals page. The top banner reads "FREE Shipping & 60-Day Money Back Satisfaction Guaranteed" — pure commerce signal. Below that: discount badges, product categories, bundles. There is no question. No educational entry point. No curiosity gesture. The experience assumes the consumer has already decided and is now choosing between SKUs. This is the most consequential strategic gap in Oral-B's portfolio: it leads the global smart toothbrush innovation race, but its discovery experience says save money, not brush better.
Deals above the fold
Zero curiosity architecture
Assumes intent, not questions
The Pattern Across All Five
Curiosity first — conversion follows
Answer-led brands convert too. Often better. The distinction isn't commercial intent — it's sequencing. Colgate asks "what do you need?" before it recommends anything. That question builds the confidence that drives the purchase. Oral-B skips the question entirely and goes straight to the discount. One earns the sale. One demands it.
Education isn't the same as answers
Sensodyne and Crest both have educational content — but it sits inside commercial architecture. True answer leadership means the exploration experience is not subordinated to the conversion pathway. The funnel and the answer cannot be the same thing.
AI mirrors what brands build
AI engines trained on these experiences will encode the same posture. A brand that says "here is a deal" online will be encoded as a commerce brand in AI answers. A brand that says "what do you need?" will be encoded as a knowledge brand. The homepage is the brief.

This study is part of the Answer Share series — category benchmarks showing how AI engines answer consumer questions about brands. VVR methodology: Visibility · Vulnerability · Readiness.
